data.title: RTX wins $441.6M PATRIOT GEM-T missile contract boost deal
data.description:
• RTX (RTX) received a $441.6M contract modification for procurement of PATRIOT GEM-T missiles supporting Operation Epic Fury.
  • The modification brings the total contract value to $441.6M, fully aligned to this award tranche.
  • The work will be executed in Chambersburg, Pennsylvania, with completion expected by Sept. 30, 2026.
  • The full $441.6M in FY26 special funds was obligated at award; the contract is being handled by Army Contracting Command.

data.title: IRadimed outlines 2026 revenue of $91M-$96M as 3870 pump ASP lift trends near 20%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: IRadimed (IRMD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We once again have a very positive performance to announce with the first quarter 2026 revenue of $22 million, a 13% increase over the first quarter of 2025," said (Founder, Chairman, CEO & President Roger Susi), adding that results reflected "solid execution across our product lines" and that revenue was "substantially derived from the... 3860 pump system," alongside "growing revenue support for our Ferro-magnetic Detection System."

  • (CEO Susi) updated early 3870 pricing and configuration trends: "we had anticipated that the 3870 pump deal typically its ASP would increase by some 10% to 14%... However... initial quoting and actual orders are showing a lift closer to 20% of 3870 ASP." He also said, "we are seeing that a majority of this new business is for Quad, 4 pump systems rather than simply replacing the older 3860 dual-channel system."

  • (CEO Susi) framed the near-term 3860-to-3870 transition as a ramp rather than a sudden uplift: "it will not be a step-change, but rather a controlled ramp," with "declining revenue derived from the older 3860 pump system... offset positively by increasing revenue from the new 3870 system as we ramp up its production."

  • "For the 3 months ended March 31, 2026, revenue was $22 million," said (CFO & Corporate Secretary John Glenn), detailing mix and profitability: "Gross Profit for the quarter was $16.8 million with a Gross Margin of 77%" and "Net income was $5.8 million or $0.45 per diluted share on a GAAP basis."

Outlook

  • "For the second quarter of 2026, we expect Revenue of $20 million to $21 million, GAAP diluted earnings per share of $0.40 to $0.44, and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $0.44 to $0.48," said (CFO Glenn).

  • "For the full year 2026, we reaffirm our guidance with a Revenue of $91 million to $96 million, GAAP diluted earnings per share of $1.90 to $2.05, and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $2.06 to $2.21," (CFO Glenn) said.

  • Versus the prior quarter’s launch timeline language, (CEO Susi) emphasized earlier-stage commercial execution in the U.S.: "we did not launch our sales effort for 3870 until late January" and said Q2 will be constrained by shipments even as orders build: "Q2 Revenue will not fully reflect this high level of exciting order activity."

Financial Results

  • (CFO Glenn) reported segment contributions for Q1 2026, including "IV infusion pump systems contributed $7.7 million" and "Patient vital signs monitoring systems contributed $7.1 million," while "Disposable revenue was $4.9 million" and "Ferro-magnetic Detection System contributed $600,000."

  • (CFO Glenn) highlighted expense and cash flow items: "Total Operating Expenses for the quarter were $9.6 million" and "Cash flow from operations was $8.3 million for the quarter," adding "Non-GAAP free cash flow was $7.8 million... after capital expenditures of approximately $500,000."

  • (CFO Glenn) noted capital return and balance sheet position: "We ended the quarter with cash and cash equivalents of $56.4 million" and "the company's Board of Directors declared a regular quarterly cash dividend of $0.20 per share... payable on May 29, 2026."

Q&A

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, Research Division: asked why customers are shifting to Quad configurations and whether it is widespread; (CEO Susi) replied, "more than half of these orders we've taken so far have been for this Quad systems," attributing it to customer reaction to the demonstrated setup: "we walk in and we're showing the Quad Stak..." and said order timing has been faster: "orders that we're getting in at this point have been rather quick."

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, Research Division: asked whether Quad orders include better per-pump pricing; (CEO Susi) said, "No," and provided pricing context: "the list price of the previous pump was $20,000" and "the Quad systems... each coming in at more than $100,000."

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, Research Division: asked what drives the plan to add another 1,000 replacement channels per year and whether greenfield is included; (CEO Susi) said, "I'm just talking taking 1,000 out of the installed-base" and added, "Greenfield will be extra," while also stating, "I don't see that we're going to have time to start calling on the greenfield for a while."

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets, LLC, Research Division: asked about manufacturing and scaling; (CEO Susi) said, "we're trying to take it a bit cautiously," reiterating "130, 135 pumps for this quarter" and describing an intended cadence: "third quarter, we'll plan to double that up again" while emphasizing execution needs: "stabilizing the supply chain."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive and oriented around upside capture and execution capacity, including: "How are you feeling from that standpoint?" and "What are the drivers to that?"

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and reinforced confidence around early demand, while tempering near-term revenue translation: (CEO Susi) said interest has been "very gratifying" and called the order pace "a frenzy right now," while also cautioning, "Q2 Revenue will not fully reflect" the order activity.

  • Compared with the previous quarter’s tone emphasizing pilot feedback and an April ramp, the current call used more definitive early-commercial language, including "a majority of this new business is for Quad" and "bookings with greater-than-expected ASP," while repeating near-term constraint framing around production ramp pacing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The 3870 narrative shifted from Q4 2025 expectations of ASP "increase 10% to 14%" and planned initial shipment timing to Q1 2026 evidence of early pricing and mix: (CEO Susi) said current quoting and orders show "a lift closer to 20%" and that "more than half" of early orders are Quad configurations.

  • Guidance framing remained centered on a second-half 2026 acceleration, but the current quarter added sharper language around Q2 revenue pressure from the 3860 decline and 3870 ramp: "keeps Q2 Revenue somewhat in check."

  • Analyst focus narrowed in Q1 2026 to 3870 ordering patterns and manufacturing ramp, versus Q4 2025 when questions also covered gross margin trajectory, disposables/services growth, inventory transition management, and international regulatory timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • (CEO Susi) described a key near-term operational constraint as production capacity and ramp discipline: "The sales team would like us to ship over 200, but we just can't do it," and said the company is prioritizing execution: "We're going to get them right."

  • (CEO Susi) flagged supply chain stabilization as part of the ramp risk: "it's a matter of ramping up the know-how and stabilizing the supply chain."

  • (CFO Glenn) described tax rate variability driven by timing: "The increase in the effective tax rate... was largely due to the timing of deductions tied to the windfall deduction for equity grants," adding, "we anticipate that the rate will trend down."

Final Takeaway

IRadimed management positioned the Q1 2026 quarter as steady performance led by pumps and monitoring, while portraying the 3870 launch as the primary stock-moving catalyst: early orders are showing an ASP lift closer to 20% and a higher-than-expected mix of Quad system purchases. Management simultaneously tempered near-term expectations by calling Q2 a controlled production ramp that will not fully reflect order momentum, while reaffirming full-year 2026 guidance and emphasizing supply chain stabilization and measured scaling as the operational priorities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Casella outlines 2026 guidance of $473M-$483M adjusted EBITDA following $150M in annualized 2026 acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Casella Waste Systems (CWST) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are very pleased with our performance in the first quarter and the strong start it provides for 2026," said "we combined disciplined positive pricing, steady core operations and meaningful acquisition activity to position the business for a strong year" (President, CEO & Director Ned Coletta).
  • "Revenues for the quarter were $457.3 million or up 9.6% year-over-year," and "solid waste pricing was up 5.1% overall" (President Coletta).
  • "Adjusted EBITDA increased 12.3% year-over-year," and the company "delivered 50 basis points of margin expansion in the quarter" (President Coletta).
  • "We have completed 4 acquisitions so far in 2026, representing approximately $150 million of annualized revenues," including "the Star Waste acquisition" that "adds approximately $100 million of annualized revenues" (President Coletta).
  • "We are on track to cut $5 million of operating costs in 2026 and another $10 million over the next 2 years" in the Mid-Atlantic integration (President Coletta).
  • "We are on track with our previously announced $15 million in targeted G&A savings over the next 3 years" (President Coletta).
  • "We continue to make great permitting progress" with "the Hakes permit expected by the third quarter of 2026" and "the Hyland permit expected by the first quarter of 2027"; at Hyland, Casella is "working to more than double the annual permit...from 460,000 tons a year to 1 million tons a year" (President Coletta).
  • "Revenues in the first quarter were $457.3 million, up $40.2 million or 9.6% year-over-year," with "$23.9 million from acquisitions" and "$16.2 million from same-store growth" (Executive VP & CFO Bradford Helgeson).

Outlook

  • "We updated financial guidance for 2026 to reflect acquisitions closed to date," raising revenue guidance to "$2.06 billion to $2.08 billion," adjusted EBITDA to "$473 million to $483 million," and adjusted free cash flow to "$200 million to $210 million" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "Guidance further assumes adjusted EBITDA margins of approximately 20%," and the revision "reflects approximately $120 million of new annualized revenue for 9 months of the year" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "We've not yet increased our guidance for the base business after the first quarter" and "we'll reevaluate guidance in future quarters" (Executive VP Helgeson).

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted net income was $12.8 million...or $0.20 per diluted share" and "GAAP net income was lower by $0.7 million...on higher acquisition expenses and additional costs associated with the organics facility closure" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "Net cash provided by operating activities was $62.3 million" and "adjusted free cash flow was $30.7 million" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "Capital expenditures were $50 million" and "DSO was 34 days" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • "As of March 31, we had $1.16 billion of debt and $127 million of cash" with covenant leverage at "2.29x"; "on a pro forma basis" after April 1 acquisitions, leverage was "approximately 2.75x" (Executive VP Helgeson).

Q&A

  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: "should we be thinking about a period of outsized margin expansion over the next couple of years?" Executive VP Helgeson: "we do see an above-trend margin improvement opportunity over the next 2 to 3 years."
  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: "the closure on Ontario could work out to be EBITDA neutral"—"can you just talk about the moving pieces there?" President Coletta: "we plan to close the Ontario landfill on December 31, 2028," and "we should be pretty neutral during that period" while "on an operating income basis, we'll actually come out the other side with a benefit."
  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: "provide an update on where we are along the landfill gas program?" President Coletta: "we have 4 projects online today" and "we expect this year several million dollars of EBITDA"; "the Waga projects, in particular, appear to be operating very well" but "we're a little early to start calling the exact production levels."
  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: asked about Star Waste margins and integration. Executive VP Helgeson: "our assumption...is about 20% EBITDA margin" and management expects to "get those margins up materially over time." President Coletta: "This is not a fix it one."
  • Patrick Brown, Raymond James: asked about the increase in landfill pricing and Q2 margin setup. President Coletta said the company "didn't have enough management of pipeline" and cited a rail move that had been pressuring the market but is now "full." Executive VP Helgeson: "It's not crazy to think" Q2 could be "slightly down...year-over-year basis, up sequentially though."
  • James Schumm, TD Cowen: raised investor concern that "landfill pricing is going to be depressed longer term." President Coletta: pricing has "generally been mid-single digits" over the last decade, and "the more expensive solution...is to move it via rail."
  • Tami Zakaria, JPMorgan: asked about CPI impacts. Executive VP Helgeson: "70%, 75% of our collection business is open market." President Coletta: "we're trying to be really dynamic" on pricing.
  • Harold Antor, Jefferies: asked why pricing can remain near current levels and about churn. Executive VP Helgeson said the company reports "a net number of rollbacks" and expects Mid-Atlantic pricing to be a "tailwind" post-integration; churn was not quantified in the transcript.
  • William Grippin, Barclays: asked about leverage capacity after Star. Executive VP Helgeson: "at 2.75x, we do have capacity" and "we have about $500 million of available liquidity."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone slightly negative to skeptical on long-term landfill pricing durability and near-term margins, including concerns that pricing was "sub-5%" (James Schumm, TD Cowen) and that Q2 margins could be pressured (Patrick Brown, Raymond James).
  • Management tone slightly positive and execution-focused in prepared remarks (President Coletta: "very pleased"), and confident but measured in Q&A, including "we do see an above-trend margin improvement opportunity" (Executive VP Helgeson) and "we're a little early to start calling the exact production levels" on RNG (President Coletta).
  • Versus last quarter’s CEO-transition framing, the current call emphasized operating execution, acquisition integration, and updated guidance; Q&A remained centered on Mid-Atlantic synergies, landfill pricing, and capital allocation in both quarters.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased in Q1 2026 to revenue of "$2.06 billion to $2.08 billion" and adjusted EBITDA of "$473 million to $483 million" (Executive VP Helgeson), compared with the prior quarter’s initial 2026 guidance of revenue "$1.97 billion to $1.99 billion" and adjusted EBITDA "$455 million to $465 million" (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • Acquisition messaging shifted from pipeline discussion in Q4 2025 to closed-deal impact in Q1 2026, including "4 acquisitions so far in 2026" and Star Waste (President Coletta).
  • Landfill pricing discussion shifted from Q4 2025 commentary that rail moves had "put a little bit of a lid on pricing" (President Coletta, prior call) to Q1 2026 commentary that the rail move discussed is "full" and the company rebuilt its landfill sales leadership (President Coletta).

Risks and Concerns

  • Weather-related softness was cited as "slightly negative volumes mainly due to the challenging winter weather" (President Coletta).
  • Commodity price pressure persisted, with "average recycled commodity revenue per ton...down 22%" year-over-year, though management said the net revenue impact was "only about $1 million" due to fee structures (Executive VP Helgeson).
  • Acquisition margin dilution remained a near-term factor, as new acquisitions "diluted margins by 15 basis points" (Executive VP Helgeson), with integration and synergies framed as the mitigation.
  • RNG execution risk with third-party developers was described as "mixed results" and "far longer" timelines (President Coletta).

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a broad-based execution quarter, pairing solid waste pricing and base-business margin expansion with a step-up in acquisitions led by Star Waste, and then raised full-year 2026 guidance to reflect deals closed to date. Leadership reiterated multi-year cost and synergy targets in the Mid-Atlantic and G&A, while highlighting permitting milestones at Hakes and Hyland and framing Ontario’s planned 2028 closure as potentially EBITDA-neutral with improved operating income profile over time.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Origin Materials plans to sell technology, wind down operations
data.description:
Origin Materials (ORGN) down 25.5% post-market Friday after saying its board determined it is in the best interests of its shareholders to sell its PET cap technology and other remaining assets, followed by an orderly wind down of operations.

Origin (ORGN) said its board approved the dissolution and liquidation of the company, subject to shareholder approval, and will call a special shareholder meeting to seek approval of the plan of dissolution; upon filing of the certificate of dissolution, it intends to cease trading in its common stock and warrants.

As part of an imminent workforce reduction, members of the executive team including CEO John Bissell will leave the company, with CFO and COO Matt Plavan appointed as interim CEO.

The company said it will provide an estimate of any amount that may be distributed to shareholders, but the amount actually distributable "may vary substantially from any estimate provided by the company, based on a number of factors."

data.title: Both up 7%: RIOT Platform sets earnings tone—will MSTR follow? Full story
data.description:
Bitcoin-linked stocks RIOT Platforms (RIOT) and Strategy (MSTR) rallied nearly 7% on May 1 as Bitcoin (BTC-USD) climbed back towards the $77K level. 

But the bigger question here is—did Riot Platform's earnings just set the tone for what Strategy could deliver next?

Riot Platforms reported Q1 revenue of $167.2M, up 3.6% Y/Y and beating analyst estimates by nearly $37.2M. However, GAAP EPS came in at -$1.44 vs. -$0.83 expected, hurt by a $326.7M non-cash Bitcoin revaluation loss and $97.7M depreciation.

Even though operating strength stood out. CFO Jamie Chung highlighted $21M in power credits, helping lower its Bitcoin mining cost to $44.6K per asset.

The company mined 1,473 Bitcoins in Q1, down 3.7% Y/Y, while holdings fell 18% to 15,680 BTC. Still, the company's average operating hash rate rose 23% to 36.4 EH/s.

A key upside was diversification—Riot Platform's data center revenue hit $33.2M in Q1, while Advanced Micro Devices doubled its capacity at its Rockdale site to 50 MW, strengthening its AI story.

Analyst sentiment also remains strong. Seeking Alpha analysts rate Riot Platforms a “Buy” with a 4.00 score, while Wall Street analysts maintain a stronger 4.57 “Strong Buy” rating.

This optimism also lifted Strategy ahead of its May 5 Q1 earnings report. Analysts expect the firm to report Q1 EPS of -18.98 on revenue of $125.1M. 

However, estimate revisions remain weak, with 0 EPS upgrades vs. 2 downgrades and 3 revenue upgrades vs. 1 downgrade.

Wall Street still keeps faith in the story, with a strong buy rating of 4.73, but the setup is simple: Riot Platforms already showed how crypto stocks react to Bitcoin strength—now the market is waiting to see if Strategy can follow the same path or break from it.

data.title: Moderna expects up to 10% 2026 revenue growth and $4.5B-$5B year-end cash, while targeting ~$4.2B cash costs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Moderna (MRNA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Moderna described Q1 performance as driven by international execution, with CEO Stéphane Bancel saying, "In the first quarter, we grew year-over-year revenues significantly to $0.4 billion, driven primarily by execution of our long-term strategic partnership in -- with the U.K. government" and adding, "With a strong Q1, we are reiterating up to 10% growth in 2026."
  • Bancel highlighted new EU regulatory milestones and their timing, stating, "we achieved an important milestone with the approval of mCOMBRIAX in the European Union" and "We also secured approval for mNEXSPIKE in the European Union," adding, "These 2 new approved products in Europe will be important growth drivers in the EU in 2027."
  • CFO James Mock emphasized Q1 revenue mix and near-term revenue expectations, saying, "For the first quarter, total revenue was $400 million" and "For the second quarter, we are expecting revenue of between $50 million and $100 million."
  • Mock detailed the Arbutus/Genevant settlement mechanics and uncertainty on the additional payment, stating, "we will make a lump sum payment of $950 million in the third quarter of 2026" and "If, however, the Federal Circuit affirms liability under Section 1498, Moderna has agreed to make an additional payment of up to $1.3 billion."
  • President Stephen Hoge framed the multi-year plan around partnerships and 2027 EU opportunity, saying, "we expect up to 10% revenue growth driven by our long-term strategic partnerships in the United Kingdom, Canada and Australia, and supported by the continued growth of mNEXSPIKE" and describing Europe as "the large $1.8 billion annual European respiratory vaccines market."

Outlook

  • Mock reiterated the 2026 framework and key assumptions, stating, "we expect total revenue to grow up to 10% in 2026" and "this guidance assumes no revenue from our flu vaccine or mCOMBRIAX."
  • Mock updated cost of sales guidance due to litigation, saying, "Our cost of sales projection has increased from $0.9 billion to $1.8 billion, and now includes the $0.9 billion litigation settlement charge."
  • Mock maintained cost targets and year-end liquidity outlook, stating, "we are expecting total GAAP operating expenses of $4.9 billion and cash costs of $4.2 billion" and "we expect to end 2026 with between $4.5 billion to $5 billion of cash and investments."
  • Compared with the prior call’s U.S. flu posture, management’s language shifted from Q4’s "disappointment" around the FDA process to Q1’s U.S. timing clarity, with Bancel saying, "In the U.S., our seasonal flu vaccine mRNA-1010 was assigned a PDUFA date of August 5."

Financial results

  • Mock reported Q1 revenue and a cost-of-sales impact tied to the settlement, stating, "revenue was $400 million in the quarter" and "Cost of sales for the quarter was $955 million. This includes $878 million related to our previously disclosed litigation settlement."
  • Mock described operating expense reductions, saying, "R&D expenses for the quarter were $649 million" and "SG&A expenses for the quarter were $173 million."
  • Mock quantified GAAP and non-GAAP loss views and liquidity, stating, "Net loss for the quarter was $1.3 billion or $3.40 per share" and "Excluding this item, the net loss would have been $0.5 billion or $1.18 per share" and "We ended the first quarter with cash and investments of $7.5 billion."

Q&A

  • Salveen Richter, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: "you newly disclosed initiation of a Phase III study for Intismeran as monotherapy"; President Hoge: "we're looking at, therefore, INT as monotherapy as opposed to just surgery and watchful waiting for high-risk Stage I disease."
  • Jessica Fye, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: "is it possible this is a double order year?"; President Hoge: "a second campaign is planned for the fall, and so the third and fourth quarter of this year."
  • Terence Flynn, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: "can you refine that at all" on melanoma interim timing; President Hoge: "we won't refine that guidance" and "There is not a built-in futility assessment."
  • Luca Issi, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: "walk us through why the legal team deemed the additional $1.3 billion charge on 1498 is not probable"; CFO Mock: "we're not really going to comment too much on the merits of the trial" and "perhaps late 2027, maybe into 2028 is where we think that this might be resolved."
  • Tyler Van Buren, TD Cowen, Research Division: "what constitutes success" in adjuvant melanoma; President Hoge: "we think the opportunity for benefit could be anywhere between that 0.5 that we saw in a number like 0.8."
  • Eliana Merle, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: "what your expectations are for timing for data from RCC and muscle invasive bladder cancer"; President Hoge: "it's hard to guide right now" and "we can't guide on when that timing would be."
  • Michael Yee, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: asked whether interim implies hazard ratio differences and RCC timing; President Hoge: "we definitely have reserved alpha for both of those" and "The DSMB will look at that result and then advise us."
  • Courtney Breen, Bernstein Institutional Services LLC, Research Division: asked on INT revenue recognition and Stage I market; CFO Mock: "it will end up being that we deliver the product to Merck"; President Hoge: "almost 1/3, north of 30% of diagnoses are now earlier stage."
  • Geoffrey Meacham, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: asked other IO combinations and norovirus bar; President Hoge: "most of our focus is on the combination with the PD-1 and KEYTRUDA" and "we want to see a vaccine efficacy that's also meaningful and so north of 50%."
  • Cory Kasimov, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: asked OS necessity in melanoma; President Hoge: "RFS is a pretty good predictor."
  • Simon Baker, Rothschild & Co Redburn, Research Division: asked Q2 mix and phasing; CFO Mock: "This was largely, the majority is COVID still" and "Last year, we had $700 million of sales outside the United States."
  • Lin Tsai, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: asked BD beyond mRNA; CEO Bancel: "If we find the right opportunity to increase what we can do, we will, of course, execute" and "we don't have a pipeline problem like most companies in the industry."
  • Alexandria Hammond, Wolfe Research, LLC: asked EU combo commercialization; President Hoge: "we do not expect revenues in the year of approval" and "We do hope for a successful launch in '27."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-skeptical, pressing on regulatory/legal uncertainty and timing, including "why the legal team deemed the additional $1.3 billion charge on 1498 is not probable" (Luca Issi, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division) and repeated requests to narrow timing like "can you refine that at all" (Terence Flynn, Morgan Stanley, Research Division).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more guarded on specifics in Q&A, with President Hoge saying, "we won't refine that guidance" and CFO Mock saying, "we're not really going to comment too much on the merits of the trial."
  • Versus Q4, the flu-related tone moved from uncertainty to process clarity, shifting from Q4’s "disappointment" framing to Q1’s "PDUFA date of August 5" (CEO Bancel) and "we would describe that as a pretty normal course" (President Hoge).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest change from Q4 was the U.S. flu regulatory status moving from "FDA Refusal-to-File" (Q4, CEO Bancel) to "assigned a PDUFA date of August 5" (Q1, CEO Bancel), alongside EU approvals for mCOMBRIAX and mNEXSPIKE becoming concrete Q1 milestones.
  • Financial framing shifted to incorporate the Arbutus/Genevant settlement into 2026 guidance, with CFO Mock updating, "Our cost of sales projection has increased from $0.9 billion to $1.8 billion."
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q4’s emphasis on flu RTF implications toward Q1 concentration on INT trial design/interims and the litigation timeline, including "late 2027, maybe into 2028" (CFO Mock).

Risks and concerns

  • Litigation remains a key uncertainty, with CFO Mock stating, "If... the Federal Circuit affirms liability under Section 1498, Moderna has agreed to make an additional payment of up to $1.3 billion" and adding, "We have concluded that a loss related to this pending Section 1498 proceeding is not probable."
  • COVID demand pressure remains embedded in guidance assumptions, with Mock saying, "Our 2026 revenue guidance factors in potential future declines in COVID vaccination rates."
  • Regulatory timing and market access execution in Europe were framed as gating items for combo uptake, with President Hoge saying market access is "pricing and reimbursement" and noting that in Europe "that takes a couple of years as a process."

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 as an internationally driven revenue quarter anchored by U.K. partnership deliveries, reiterated "up to 10%" 2026 revenue growth and a ~$4.2B adjusted cash cost objective, and positioned EU approvals of mCOMBRIAX and mNEXSPIKE as 2027 growth drivers. The call also centered investor attention on the Arbutus/Genevant settlement’s cash timing and the Section 1498 appeal path, while Q&A concentrated on the expanding Intismeran Phase III program design and event-driven readout timing across oncology and norovirus.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BlackRock Flexible Income ETF declares monthly distribution of $0.2201
data.description:
• BlackRock Flexible Income ETF (BINC) - $0.2201.
  • Payable May 06; for shareholders of record May 01; ex-div May 01.

data.title: Vanda rises after hours on launch of tradipitant for motion sickness
data.description:
• Vanda Pharmaceuticals (VNDA) is up ~3% in after-hours trading Friday after announcing the availability of Nereus (tradipitant) for the prevention of vomiting induced by motion in adults.
  • The company said that Nereus is the first new drug treatment for motion sickness in more than four decades.
  • Nereus is an oral neurokinin-1 (NK-1) receptor antagonist.
  • Vanda said through a website, cash pay patients could get the drug for $85 per capsule.

data.title: GameStop preparing offer for eBay - WSJ
data.description:
GameStop (GME) is preparing to make an offer for eBay (EBAY), reported The Wall Street Journal on Friday.

According to the article, citing people familiar with the matter, GameStop has been quietly building a stake in eBay’s shares ahead of a potential offer.

eBay, which has a market cap of over $46B, rose 10.2% in extended trading.

GameStop could submit an offer for eBay as soon as later this month, WSJ said. 

If eBay isn’t receptive, GameStop CEO Ryan Cohen could decide to take the offer directly to eBay’s shareholders, the report added, citing one of the people.

GameStop and eBay did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

data.title: Advanced Gold Exploration names Jason Baker as new CFO
data.description:
• Advanced Gold Exploration announced on Friday that, further to his role as director of the company, Jason Baker has been appointed CFO, replacing David MacDonald.
  • Jason Baker is a finance professional at Altus Capital Partners and has helped raise over $35M in equity financings over the past two years for companies including Scorpio Gold (SRCRF) and Magma Silver (MGMA:CA).
  • Source: Press Release
